body.label: thm:projection_shift_invertible

hypothesis.statement: Assume that V is a finite-dimensional vector space.
Assume that T : V → V is a linear map satisfying T^2 = T.
Let λ be a scalar with λ ≠ 0 and λ ≠ 1.
Define S = T - λ I, where I : V → V is the identity map.

body.claim: The linear map S = T - λ I is invertible.

proof.claim: For every v ∈ V we have T(T(v)) = T(v), and in particular for every v in im(T) we have T(v) = v.
For every v ∈ V, if v ∈ im(T) then T(v) = v.
proof.proof_method: apply T to v = T(u) for some u ∈ V and use T^2 = T

results_used.statement: If v = T(u) for some u ∈ V, then T(v) = T(T(u)) = T(u) = v, so T acts as the identity on im(T).

proof.claim: For every v ∈ V, the vector w := v - T(v) lies in ker(T).

results_used.statement: T(w) = T(v - T(v)) = T(v) - T(T(v)) = T(v) - T(v) = 0, hence w ∈ ker(T).

proof.claim: Every v ∈ V can be written as v = w + T(v) with w ∈ ker(T) and T(v) ∈ im(T), so V = ker(T) + im(T).
proof.proof_method: use the decomposition v = (v - T(v)) + T(v)

results_used.statement: For each v ∈ V, w = v - T(v) ∈ ker(T) and T(v) ∈ im(T), so v is a sum of an element of ker(T) and an element of im(T).

proof.proof_method: direct argument using T(v) = 0 and T(v) = v

results_used.statement: If v ∈ ker(T) ∩ im(T), then T(v) = 0 since v ∈ ker(T), and T(v) = v since v ∈ im(T); hence v = 0.

proof.proof_method: show the sum is direct and equals V

results_used.statement: V = ker(T) + im(T) and ker(T) ∩ im(T) = {0} together imply V = ker(T) ⊕ im(T).

proof.claim: For every v ∈ ker(T), S(v) = -λ v, so S acts on ker(T) as multiplication by the scalar -λ.

results_used.statement: If v ∈ ker(T), then T(v) = 0, so S(v) = (T - λ I)(v) = T(v) - λ v = -λ v.

proof.claim: The restriction of S to ker(T) is an invertible linear map from ker(T) to itself.
proof.proof_method: scalar multiplication by a nonzero scalar is invertible

results_used.statement: Since λ ≠ 0, the scalar -λ is invertible, so the map v ↦ -λ v on ker(T) is invertible.

proof.claim: For every v ∈ im(T), S(v) = (1 - λ) v, so S acts on im(T) as multiplication by the scalar 1 - λ.
proof.proof_method: direct computation using T(v) = v on im(T)

results_used.statement: If v ∈ im(T), then T(v) = v, so S(v) = (T - λ I)(v) = T(v) - λ v = v - λ v = (1 - λ) v.

proof.claim: The restriction of S to im(T) is an invertible linear map from im(T) to itself.
proof.proof_method: scalar multiplication by a nonzero scalar is invertible

results_used.statement: Since λ ≠ 1, the scalar 1 - λ is invertible, so the map v ↦ (1 - λ) v on im(T) is invertible.

proof.claim: With respect to the direct sum decomposition V = ker(T) ⊕ im(T), the map S is block-diagonal with diagonal blocks -λ · id_{ker(T)} on ker(T) and (1 - λ) · id_{im(T)} on im(T).
proof.proof_method: use the decomposition v = x + y with x ∈ ker(T), y ∈ im(T) and linearity of S

results_used.statement: For v = x + y with x ∈ ker(T), y ∈ im(T), we have S(v) = S(x) + S(y) = -λ x + (1 - λ) y.

proof.claim: There exists a linear map R : V → V such that R is both a left and right inverse of S.
proof.proof_method: define R on the direct sum decomposition and verify SR = RS = id_V

results_used.statement: For v = x + y with x ∈ ker(T), y ∈ im(T), define R(v) = -1/λ x + 1/(1 - λ) y; then S(R(v)) = v and R(S(v)) = v.

proof.claim: S is invertible as a linear map V → V.

results_used.statement: A linear map with a two-sided inverse is invertible.
